rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF VICTOR CHURCH
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Compel Remote Deposition Of Florentina Iglesias Mendioroz; Request For Recovery Of Fees And Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 4] An oral deposition in a foreign nation may be obtained under the same procedures for taking an oral deposition in California. (See, Code of Civil Procedure sections 2027.010(a) [Depositions in a foreign nation], and 2025.010.) The Opposition is correct that the deposition of a natural person who is a party must be taken (a) within 75 miles of the deponent's residence, or (b) in the [California] county where the action is pending and within 150 miles of the deponent's residence (Code of Civil Procedure section 2025.250). The party giving notice of the deposition has the choice of these options. The deposition may take place at a more distant location from the deposed party's residence, under a court order (Code of Civil Procedure section 2025.260). The argument that this Code section requires deponents in foreign nations to be deposed only in person, with the deposing party's attorney physically traveling from California to the deponent, ignores other relevant statutes allowing oral depositions to be taken remotely, and appears to confuse the intention of section 2025.250, which is to protect the deposed party from the costs and burden of traveling to California for a deposition, not to shift that same cost and burden of physical travel to the party taking the oral deposition. The Opposition also cites Code of Civil Procedure section 367.75(f) to argue the court cannot compel a party to appear remotely. Code of Civil Procedure section 367.75 applies to "conferences, hearings, and proceedings" before the court (see, sub. (a)(1)), and is not relevant to this motion to compel a party's remote attendance at a deposition conducted outside of the court's presence. The court notes in passing that Ms. Mendioroz appeared at the December 14, 2022 9:00 a.m. hearing in this matter via Zoom, with counsel. California law allows for an oral deposition to be taken by remote means. Pursuant to California Rules of Court, rule 3.1010(a)(1), "Any party may take an oral deposition by telephone, videoconference, or other remote electronic means, provided: (1) Notice is served with the notice of deposition or the subpoena[.]" Rule 3.1010 was adopted to establish implementing procedures for Code of Civil Procedure section 2025.310. Under Code of Civil Procedure section 2025.310(a), "At the election of the deponent or the deposing party, the deposition officer may attend the deposition at a different location than the deponent via remote means. A deponent is not required to be physically present with the deposition officer when being sworn in at the time of the deposition." In other words, the Discovery Act does not require the deponent party and the attorney conducting the deposition to participate from the same location. An oral deposition may be taken "by telephone, videoconference, or other remote electronic means," so long as the oral deposition is properly noticed as such under the statute. Under Code of Civil Procedure sections 2027.010(b), "If a deponent is a party to the action [ ] the service of the deposition notice is effective to compel the deponent to attend and to testify, as well as to produce any document, electronically stored information, or tangible thing for inspection, copying, testing, or sampling." (Code of Civil Procedure section 2027.010.)

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF VICTOR CHURCH
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion To Compel Remote Deposition Of Florentina Iglesias Mendioroz; Request For Recovery Of Fees And Sanctions (Added For The Purpose Of Posting Tentative Ruling).
rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 4] However, there is no showing on the record now before the court that the moving party, Mr. Iglesias, has satisfied the statutory threshold requirement of the laws allowing oral deposition of a party to the action located in a foreign nation to be taken by remote means. There is no showing on the record that Mr. Iglesias served Ms. Mendioroz a notice of deposition, with notice of the remote means of deposition (see, Code of Civil Procedure sections 2025.010, 2025.310, 2027.010; CRC, rule 3.1010). And, although CRC 3.1010(c), cited by moving party, does state "A deponent must appear as required by statute or as agreed to by the parties and deponent" there is no showing that appearance "as required by statute" is currently applicable here. The moving party does not offer authority regarding how the alleged" substantial compliance with the deposition notice [ ] requirement which generally preambles a Motion to Compel" (Reply at 2:22-24) applies to the required service of notice of deposition under the statutes discussed above. Further, as to appearance at a deposition "as agreed to by the parties and deponent," based on the record before the court, counsel had agreed to a date, time, and means for Ms. Mendioroz's deposition in July 2023, but as of the January 25, 2024 email from Gamez to Yu, there is now no agreement among the parties about Ms. Mendioroz's appearance at a deposition. Petitioner Victor Iglesias' Motion To Compel Remote Deposition Of Florentina Iglesias Mendioroz, and related requests for sanctions, is DENIED. Ms. Mendioroz's request in the Opposition papers for a protective order pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 2025.420 is DENIED as procedurally defective (see, Code of Civil Procedure section 2025.420), as well as premature. Ms. Mendioroz's request for monetary sanctions pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 2025.480(j) is DENIED. Procedurally, the Motion To Compel Remote Deposition Of Florentina Iglesias Mendioroz is not brought pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 2025.480. The Opposition does not argue why this court should award sanctions under a statute the motion does not cite in seeking relief. On its own, the court finds the circumstances would make imposition of sanctions on either party unjust. Counsel for both parties have presented legally defective theories to support their arguments on this motion practice, and their clients can bear the costs thereby incurred. The First District Court of Appeal stated in Clement v. Alegre (2009) 177 Cal.App.4th 1277, a dispute over the award of discovery sanctions, Twenty-three years ago, the Legislature enacted the Civil Discovery Act of 1986 (Code Civ. Proc., sec. 2016.010 et seq.)1 (the Act), a comprehensive revision of pretrial discovery statutes, the central precept of which is that civil discovery be essentially self-executing. More than 10 years ago, Townsend v. Superior Court (1998) 61 Cal.App.4th 1431 [ ] lamented the all too often interjection of "ego and emotions of counsel and clients" into discovery disputes, warning that "[l]ike Hotspur on the field of battle, counsel can become blinded by the combative nature of the proceeding and be rendered incapable of informally resolving a disagreement."2 (Id. at p. 1436.) Townsend counseled that the "informal resolution" of discovery disputes "entails something more than bickering with [opposing counsel]." (Id. at p. 1439, 72 Cal.Rptr.2d 333.) Rather, the statute "requires that there be a serious effort at negotiation and informal resolution." (Id. at p. 1438, 72 Cal.Rptr.2d 333.)

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF VICTOR CHURCH
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion To Compel Remote Deposition Of Florentina Iglesias Mendioroz; Request For Recovery Of Fees And Sanctions (Added For The Purpose Of Posting Tentative Ruling).
rulings.Rulings: [Part 4 of 4] Given the communications between counsel presented in this record and the cross-allegations of sharp practices in the papers, the court further suggests counsel read "California Attorney Guidelines of Civility and Professionalism," which include deposition standards (see https://www.calbar.ca.gov/Portals/0/documents/ethics/Civility/Atty-Civility-Guide-Revised_Sept-2014.pdf). An attorney should remember that vigorous advocacy can be consistent with professional courtesy, and that arguments or conflicts with other counsel should not be personal. An attorney should treat other counsel and participants with courtesy and civility and should not engage in conduct that would be inappropriate in the presence of a judicial officer. Pursuant to CRC 3.1010(d), the court orders the parties to meet and confer in good faith, in person or telephonically and not over email or letter, to determine (1) the means by which Ms. Mendioroz's deposition will be taken, as a party to the action, (2) the date or dates the deposition will be notice for and held, based on the mutual availability of counsel, Ms. Mendioroz, and any other party necessary or authorized to attend; (3) the beginning and end times of the deposition or depositions, specified in terms of all relevant local time zones (i.e. both California and Spain); (4) the presence at the location of the deponent of all translators, reporters, and technical support persons. Petitioner Victor Iglesias shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling as published above. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF VICTOR CHURCH
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion To Compel Remote Deposition Of Florentina Iglesias Mendioroz; Request For Recovery Of Fees And Sanctions (Added For The Purpose Of Posting Tentative Ruling).
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 4] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Stephen M. Murphy. Before the court is Petitioner Victor Iglesias' Motion To Compel Remote Deposition Of Florentina Iglesias Mendioroz. The instant Motion was filed February 22, 2024. Victor Iglesias and Florentina Iglesias Mendioroz are opposing parties to the Will Contest in this matter. Mr. Iglesias lives in Los Angeles, California. Ms. Mendioroz lives in Spain. Discussion: Opposing party's Request for Judicial notice of certain court records is GRANTED, under Evid. C. section 452(d). Per the Notice of Motion, the moving party is seeking "an order compelling Florentina Iglesias Mendioroz to attend and testify via Zoom at her deposition by Mr. Iglesias in this case, as well as recovery of fees and sanctions." Per the moving papers and the February 13, 2024 Joint Case Management Statement, the parties had informally agreed to specific August 2023 deposition dates for Mr. Iglesias and Ms. Mendioroz in July 2023, following mediation efforts (see, Ex. F to MPA and Ex. C to Decl. of Gamez ISO Opposition; Joint Case Mgmt. Stmnt at 5:20-24). In seeking the court's intervention in this dispute, the moving party relies on the related assertions that there has been "substantial compliance with the deposition notice [ ] requirement which generally preambles a Motion to Compel[ ]" (Reply at 2:22-24), and that the parties mutually stipulated to remote oral depositions via counsels' written communication in July and August of 2023, specifically the July 17, 2023 email from attorney Gamez to attorney Yu. The email states in relevant part "I am writing to confirm that we have agreed to the following dates for depositions: August 18: Victor Iglesias by Zoom beginning at 8 am. August 24: Florentina Iglesias Mendioroz by Zoom beginning at 8 am." (Ex. F to MPA and Ex. C to Decl. of Gamez ISO Opposition). As shown in the communication between counsel submitted in support and opposition of this motion, those specified dates were canceled in subsequent communications. Counsel for the parties were able to reschedule a mutually agreeable date for Mr. Iglesias' deposition, which was formally noticed and taken in San Francisco on February 7, 2024. Simultaneously, as demonstrated in the voluminous communications between counsel dated between July 2023 and February 2024 submitted in the moving and opposition papers, counsels' communications regarding scheduling Ms. Mendioroz's deposition eventually degraded into a dispute over how the deposition would be conducted: remotely, with Ms. Mendioroz appearing from Spain over Zoom; or in person and within 75 miles of Ms. Mendioroz's residence, in Spain. (See, Exhs. C, D, E to MPA; Ex. A, B, and G of Decl. of Gamez ISO Opposition). As of the January 25, 2024 email from Gamez to Yu, and the February 18, 2024 letter from Gamez to Yu in response to the February 13, 2024 letter from Yu to Gamez, and in the Opposition to this Motion, Ms. Mendioroz had taken the position that the law does not allow Mr. Iglesias to depose her remotely, and that she must be deposed in person within 75 miles of her residence in Spain, citing CCP section 2027.010 and 2025.310.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF ALEKSANDER ALEKSANDROVICH DONSKOY AKA ALEKSANDER ALEKSANDROVICH DONSKOY AKA DONSKOI ALEKSANDER ALEKSAND
rulings.Calendar Matter: PETITION FOR SPOUSAL PROPERTY
rulings.Rulings: Hearing. Appearance required. 1) The Court is to determine whether to grant the objector's request for judicial notice of the Russian district court's judgment dated 11/15/2021 and Russian regional court's appellate ruling dated 03/10/2022 (see Evidence Code section 452(f)) to find that: i) The Russian Marital Contract continues to govern property characterization after either spouse/contracting party's death; and ii) Under the Marital Contract provisions, the assets of this estate are entirely separate property of the decedent subject to his testamentary disposition. 2) The Court is to determine whether California law governs procedural aspects of this ancillary probate, while Russian law controls substantive issues (beneficiaries entitled to distribution and their shares). If California law controls, the petitioner's 50% share of the inventoried assets should qualify as property passing to her as the decedent's surviving spouse without ancillary administration under Probate Code section 13500, in the absence of the personal representative's petition under section 13502.5 to compel administration of the petitioner's 50% share of the inventoried assets. 3) The Court is to determine whether the personal representative should be directed or authorized to withhold the petitioner's share of the US estate tax from 50% of the inventoried assets passing to the petitioner without ancillary administration, in the absence of the personal representative's petition under section 20120 to determine the proration of the US estate tax. PARTIES APPEARING SHOULD CHECK IN FOR THE HEARING WITH THEIR FULL NAME. Counsel and parties may appear personally or remotely through Zoom or CourtCall. For a video appearance, go to zoom.us, click "join a meeting" and input meeting ID 160 225 4765 and password 514879. For an audio-only appearance through Zoom, call 1-669-254-5252 and key in meeting ID "160 225 4765#", then participant ID "0#", then password "514879#". (Toll rates may apply).) The Court prefers that counsel and parties appearing by video input their first and last name into the "Your Name" dialogue box. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling 1-888-882-6878. Please review LRSF 14.1(C) for more information.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF EVA C. TOLER
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Report, Fees, Final Distribution
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 5/7/24. (No earlier availability.) Issue(s) necessitating continuance: (1) The Notice of Hearing does not show the full caption of the 'Third Amended Report of the Administrator's Waiver of Account and Petition for Allowance of Statutory Compensation to Administrator and Attorney, and Compensation to Attorney for Extraordinary Services and for Final Distribution and Close the Estate and Discharge the Administrator." [CRC 7.50.] (2) The Notice of Hearing is also incomplete at item 1 (representative capacity). (3) It is unclear why a completely amended pleading was filed again (with the same errors as in the second amended petition - see items 7 - 12 below), when the only issues with the second amended petition had been the defective notice and attorney fees for extraordinary services that appeared to be duplicative and not sufficiently supported. A simple amendment to the second amended petition could have been filed to show the reduction of a few attorney hours. The slightly revised fee declarations still do not address why two attorneys needed to bill for the same services. (4) How did a net loss of $156,000 benefit the estate? (5) There is no Exhibit R to this fully amended third petition. (6) The total amount of extraordinary fees shown in paragraph Y is incorrect. (7) Probate Advance, LLC's request for special notice was filed on 4/22/22. (8) Paragraph H has still not been revised to make sense. (9) There are still references in paragraph I, J, and K to the "account" when accounting was waived. (10) Paragraph O still claims that assets did not change form during administration; the only asset of the estate was sold for cash. (11) Administrator is the sole heir. Why did he have to serve Notice of Proposed Action to himself? (12) The Court's order for a blocked account was made on 11/15/23, not 9/1/23. (13) Since the $315,079.75 proceeds from the sale of the real property were deposited in the attorney's client trust account, it did not earn interest from July 2023 to January 2024, when it was finally wired to the estate account. (14) Why is the balance in that blocked account only $315,090.97 as of 2/10/24? (15) Bank statements are not required when accounting has been waived. Deadline is 4/26/24 for providing the assigned examiner the courtesy copies of any papers filed to address these issues. Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10 B. (NB: In order for this Court to track closed matters, orders to terminate and distribute an estate must begin with the following language: "Order for Final Distribution." [LRSF 14.35 I.6.b.])

Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling 1-888-882-6878.

rulings.Case Title: CONSERVATORSHIP OF JONATHAN W. KUTCHINS
rulings.Calendar Matter: Petition For (1) For Substituted Judgment To Execute Trust And Pour Over Will; (2) Transfer Income And Assets To Trust; (3) To Bring Trust Under Continuing Court Supervision; (4) Set Trustee Bond; And (5) Authority To Maintain Current Investments
rulings.Rulings: Hearing. Appearance required. 1) The conservator requests an order immediately discharging his bond in favor of a new bond in the same amount he intends to file as trustee of the proposed trust. The conservator has not filed a first interim account showing the estate condition satisfying the waiver requirements of Probate Code sections 2323 and 2628, nor has the conservator filed a final account showing exhaustion of the estate, coupled with a petittion to terminate the conservatorship estate under section 2626. 2) The conservator requests authority to transfer the conservatee's assets to the proposed trust without inventorying and appraising them as assets initially belonging to the conservatorship estate. Section 2610(b) requires the conservator to file an inventory and appraisal listing all asstes of the conservatee of which the conservator has knowledge, regardless of whether the conservator has possession of such assets. See also CRC 7.1059(b)(7) requiring the conservator to hold title reflecting the conservatorship in the conservatee's financial accounts and certain other assets. 3) A review of the proposed order indicates that the conservator requests authority to transfer the conservatee's Fidelity account 8810 identified as "SIMPLE IRA" to the proposed trust, even though IRAs cannot be transferred to a trust. 4) The Court is to determine whether to approve the proposed action, taking into consideration Probate Code section 2583 factors and other relevant circumstances. See Conservatorship of Hart (1991) 228 Cal.App.3d 1244, 1254 ("If the superior court is to be able validly to substitute its judgment for that of the conservatee it must be given complete information as to all relevant circumstances. The conservator must bear the burden of informing the court fully and fairly. And in any case the superior court must on its own motion take all steps necessary to satisfy itself, as the conservatee's decisionmaking surrogate, that it has been fully and fairly informed.") PARTIES APPEARING SHOULD CHECK IN FOR THE HEARING WITH THEIR FULL NAME. Counsel and parties may appear personally or remotely through Zoom or CourtCall. For a video appearance, go to zoom.us, click "join a meeting" and input meeting ID 160 225 4765 and password 514879. For an audio-only appearance through Zoom, call 1-669-254-5252 and key in meeting ID "160 225 4765#", then participant ID "0#", then password "514879#". (Toll rates may apply).) The Court prefers that counsel and parties appearing by video input their first and last name into the "Your Name" dialogue box. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling 1-888-882-6878. Please review LRSF 14.1(C) for more information.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF HAYDEN CHEUKHEI KAM
rulings.Calendar Matter: PETITION FOR LETTERS OF ADMINISTRATION
rulings.Rulings: Grant without hearing. Status of closing the estate will be set for 4/8/2025. A petition for final distribution or a report of status with a petition requesting additional time for estate administration must be filed at least two weeks before that status date. Appearance will be required on 4/8/2025 if no petition has been filed.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF WILLIA CONEY CORKE
rulings.Calendar Matter: PETITION FOR LETTERS OF ADMINISTRATION
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 5/15/24. (No earlier availability.) Issue(s) necessitating continuance: (1) No proposed order has been lodged or received by the assigned examiner yet. [LRSF 14.7 A. and 14.59 E.] (2) Notice of Hearing must have attached to it the procedures for how to make a remote appearance by Zoom and CourtCall, as well as information about remote public access, as set out at https://sf.courts.ca.gov/divisions/probate-court. [LRSF 14.1 C.6.] (3) When did the decedent's spouse die? Who is his successor-in-interest if he post-deceased? (The "estate" of a deceased person is not sufficient; a personal representative must be identified. If the deceased person had a trust, the trustee must be identified.) (4) Bond is required for a non-resident personal representative, notwithstanding the unknown value of the estate. Waivers of bond have not been filed. (5) The Confidential Statement of Birth Date and Driver's License Number (Judicial Council Form DE-147S) is also required, pursuant to Probate Code Section 8404(b). Form DE-147S must be filed separately and confidentially with a cover sheet. Deadline is 5/6/24 for the proposed order and courtesy copies of any papers filed to address these issues to be received by the assigned examiner. Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10 B.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF ARNOLD YEE
rulings.Calendar Matter: PETITION FOR LETTERS OF ADMINISTRATION
rulings.Rulings: Grant without hearing.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF CECIE WEST
rulings.Calendar Matter: PETITION FOR LETTERS OF ADMINISTRATION
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 5/15/24. (No earlier availability.) Issue(s) necessitating continuance: (1) No proposed order has been lodged or received yet by the assigned examiner. [LRSF 14.7 A. and 14.59 E.] (2) The notice must have attached to it the procedures for how to make a remote appearance by Zoom and CourtCall, as well as information about remote public access, as set out at https://sf.courts.ca.gov/divisions/probate-court. [LRSF 14.1 C.6.] (3) The Acknowledgement of Receipt of the Duties and Liabilities has not yet been filed. (4) The Confidential Statement of Birth Date and Driver's License Number (Judicial Council Form DE-147S) is also required, pursuant to Probate Code Section 8404(b). Form DE-147S must be filed separately and confidentially with a cover sheet. (5) What are the dates of death of the deceased issue? [LRSF 14.18 C.] (6) If a grandchild died, Attachment 8 must clearly state whether or not he/she was survived by issue and must include those issue, if any. (7) The proof of service shows numerous addresses that are not the ones shown in the petition and includes two individuals who are not listed on Attachment 8. (No instructions re how to appear remotely were attached.) Deadline is 5/6/24 for a proposed order and courtesy copies of the petition and any papers filed to address these issues to be received by the assigned examiner. Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10 B. (NB: A petitioner seeking appointment must sign and verify her own pleadings, including any supplement or amendment to her petition. Please do NOT file a Notice of Hearing; that is not the proper form of notice at this stage in opening estate administration.)

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF JOAN LEWIS DANFORTH
rulings.Calendar Matter: PETITION FOR PROBATE OF WILL AND FOR LETTERS TESTAMENTARY
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 5/15/24. (No earlier availability.) Issue(s) necessitating continuance: (1) No proposed order has been lodged or received by the assigned examiner. [LRSF 14.7 A. and 14.59 E.] (2) Notice must have attached to it the procedures for how to make a remote appearance by Zoom and CourtCall, as well as remote public access, as set out at https://sf.courts.ca.gov/divisions/probate-court. [LRSF 14.1 C.6.] (3) When did Robert Danforth die? (4) Notwithstanding the testator's statement that she had no issue, how did the petitioner determine that she was not survived by any kin other than those of her predeceased spouse? [LRSF 14.18 C.1.] (5) Who were Charles Sauer and Patricia Lambert, whose names are on an Attachment to Notice of Hearing Proof of Service by Mail attached to the petition? If they were heirs, when did they die? [LRSF 14.18 C. and D.] Please provide a proposed order and courtesy copies of any papers filed to address these issues to the assigned examiner by 5/6/24. Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10 B.
